commentaries.author: Augustine of Hippo
commentaries.content: For this reason, after saying, "Thy thoughts are very deep," he at once subjoins: "An unwise man doth not well consider this, and a fool doth not understand it" (ver. What are the things which an unwise man cloth not well consider, and which a fool doth not understand. "When the ungodly are green as the grass. " What is, "as the grass". They flourish when it is winter, but they will wither in the summer. Thou observest the flower of the grass. What more quickly passeth by. What is brighter. What is greener. Let not its verdure delight thee, but fear its withering. Thou hast heard of the ungodly being green as the grass: hear also of the righteous: "For lo. " In the mean while, consider the ungodly; they flourish as the grass; but who are they who understand it not. The foolish and unwise. "When the ungodly are green as the grass, and all men look upon the workers of iniquity" (ver. All who in their heart think not aright of God, look upon the ungodly when they are as green as grass, that is, when they flourish for a time. Why do they look upon them. "That they may be destroyed for ever. " For they regard their momentary bloom, they imitate them, and wishing to flourish with them for a time, perish for evermore: this is, "That they may be destroyed for ever.

commentaries.author: George Leo Haydock
commentaries.content: Appear. Hebrew, "flourish. "Still they are but as grass, (Haydock) short-lived, and of small utility. The just resembles the palm-tree, ver. 13. (Calmet)

methodology.step_1: Parse main page with BeautifulSoup for structure
methodology.step_3: Use Firecrawl to get full content from 'Go to Commentary' links
methodology.step_4: Fallback to BeautifulSoup for accordion content if needed
advantages: Records verses even without commentaries for complete tracking
